figures.caption: Table 3. Quantitative comparison for object detection and instance segmentation tasks. Our S2-CoT nears full fine-tuning and outperforms other PEFT methods. Best results are in bold, second-best are underlined. See Sec. F for full Cheng2020-anchor-based results.
Figure 19. More detection qualitative results.
Figure 18. More detection qualitative results.
Figure 20. More segmentation qualitative results.
Figure 21. More segmentation qualitative results.
Figure 8. Scaled deviation map of two strategies: SFA-only and SFA+SCA.

Figure 7. Spatial correlation of (y − µ)/σ for models trained with λ=0.5. SFA+SCA (right) reduces average spatial correlation compared to SFA-only (middle) and the base codec (left), which benefits subsequent machine vision tasks.
Figure 9. Each row corresponds to a different strategy and shows the energy of five channels with the highest entropy.

Table 17. Quantitative comparison for object detection and instance segmentation tasks based on the DCAE and ELIC codec. Our method is competitive with full fine-tuning against others. Our method demonstrates superior BD-metric performance and parameter efficiency.

Figure 17. Comparison of rate-accuracy performance across various tasks and base codecs. (a) The instance segmentation results on the DCAE base codec. (b) Object detection (left) and instance segmentation (right) performance on the ELIC base codec.
Table 5. Ablation study of SFA and SCA synergy.
Figure 6. Pairwise channel similarities of the latent representation y for different fine-tuning strategies. Incorporating SCA markedly reduces the inter-channel correlation.

Table 11. Extended quantitative results for object detection and instance segmentation tasks based on the Cheng2020-anchor base codec. This table provides the full results that are referenced in the main paper. Best results are in bold, second-best are underlined.

Table 13. Ablation study on the placement of SCA within the entropy model. Gain (∆mAP) is measured as the difference in mAP between our method’s result and the value predicted by the Adapt-ICMH Rate-mAP curve at the same bitrate.
Table 15. Performance comparison. Top: compression ratio and mAP on original images. Bottom: performance on three detectors.
Figure 5. Qualitative comparison on the COCO2017 dataset.
Table 6. Ablation study on the placement sensitivity of the SFA and SCA.

Figure 2. Overview of our proposed S2-CoT. It includes the Encoder, Decoder, Entropy Model, and Task Models. The colors of the modules indicate their training status: the base codecs and downstream task models are frozen (indicated in blue), while the trainable SFA and SCA are in red and orange, respectively. Here, ⊙ denotes the Hadamard product and ⊕ denotes element-wise addition.
Table 16. More Detailed Comparison on computational complexity.
Table 10. Training hyperparameter settings for the main trials.
Figure 22. More detection qualitative results.
Figure 23. More segmentation qualitative results.

Figure 15. Our SFA and SCA are integrated into the DCAE codec [40]. Downsample denotes the ResidualBottleneckBlockWithStride blocks, where STB indicates the SwinBlockWithConvMulti blocks. Conv(5, 2↓) represents a 5×5 convolution with a stride of 2. Upsample denotes the ResidualBottleneckBlockWithUpsample blocks. Conv(5, 2↑) represents a 5× 5 convolution with a stride of 2 for upsampling.

Figure 14. Our SFA and SCA are integrated into the Cheng2020-anchor codec [10]. ResBlk denotes a residual block, where ResBlk↓ indicates a downsampling residual block with a stride of 2. Conv represents a standard 3 × 3 convolution. LReLU denotes Leaky ReLU. Sub Conv(3, 2↑) denotes a sub-pixel convolution (kernel size=3) with a stride of 2 for upsampling.

Figure 13. Our SFA and SCA are integrated into the Lu2022-TIC codec [41]. STB denotes the Swin-Transformer Block. Conv(n, 2↑) denotes a transposed convolution (kernel size=n) with a stride of 2 for upsampling, and Conv(n, 2↓) denotes a convolution (kernel size=n) with a stride of 2 for downsampling.

Figure 16. Our SFA and SCA are integrated into the ELIC codec [21]. Conv(5, 2↓) represents a 5× 5 convolution with a stride of 2. ResB denotes residual blocks, and AttB denotes attention blocks.. Conv(3, 1) represents a standard 3 × 3 convolution with a padding of 1. The deConv(5, 2↑) denotes a 5× 5 transposed convolution with a stride of 2, serving as a learnable spatial upsampling operation.

Figure 3. Comparison of rate-accuracy performance across various tasks and base codecs. (a) Object detection and instance segmentation results on the Lu2022-TIC (top row) and Cheng2020-anchor (bottom row) base codecs. (b) Classification performance on the Lu2022-TIC base codec. (c) Object detection performance on the DCAE base codec. Results for the fourth base codec ELIC are in Sec. K.
Figure 11. ResNet50-based FPN architecture, indicating the features selected for perceptual loss evaluation.
Figure 10. Object detection performance on the COCO2017 dataset using Lu2022-TIC as the base codec. Incorporating SCA reduces the bitrate without compromising detection accuracy.
